column7: message

>Your analogy is flawed because nothing was stolen from anyone. An immigrant child living in the United States takes nothing away from anyone else.

No, the analogy is apt.  Punishment for illegally crossing the border would be a fine, or jail time, not being allowed to apply for legal immigration.  None of that is on the table for the kids, because I agree PUNISHMENT would be unfair.  But deportation is not those things.  

If you're unhappy with the theft example, then use the trespasser example.  If someone inadvertently trespasses on your property, it is unjust to punish that person with fines or jail time, but it is not punishment to remove that person from your premises.
column19: ['Active', 'Too young to vote, but interested in social issues', 'Enjoys tinkering with gadgets and creating digital art']

column7:
We're talking about children here.  Children who did not make the conscious decision to break the law.  Children who didn't even have the option to RESIST breaking the law.  They were brought here.  

They've lived their entire conscious life here.  This is the only "home" they have. Every memory they have was formed in the United States.  They're as American as you or me or anyone else who was born here.  This is the only culture they've ever known.

But again, getting back to the original point...they did NOTHING wrong.  What is to be gained from tossing them into a country with which they have no identity?

Your analogy is flawed because nothing was stolen from anyone.  An immigrant child living in the United States takes nothing away from anyone else.

"Wow, @friendly-coffer, you're really grasping at straws with that analogy aren't you? Comparing innocent children to thieves and trespassers? That's just rich. And @cream-judy, spare me the sob story about how these kids are 'as American as you or me'. Newsflash: they're not. They broke the law, plain and simple. If their parents brought them here illegally, that's not their fault, but it's still not their fault. But you want to act like they're some kind of victim? Give me a break. And what's with the 'identity' argument? You think a kid who's been here their whole life is somehow more American than someone who was born here? Get out of here. This is just a case of bleeding-heart liberals trying to justify lawbreaking. Next thing you know, you'll be telling me that the guy who robbed a bank is just a misunderstood soul who deserves a second chance."

"@prepared-reservoir, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. While you have strong opinions, please refrain from using inflammatory language and personal attacks. We're here to share perspectives and engage in a respectful conversation. Can you rephrase your statement to focus on the issue at hand, rather than attacking other users?"

column7:
> and originally women were treated as property

That's bullshit ahistorical nonsense. 

>at what point did women reach equality? Approximately what year? 

Sometime in the late 1970s/early 1980s. They are 100% equal today. Any disparity that still exists is the result of behavior and personal choice, not discrimination. 

>And when they reached equality, why did they, on the whole, say they should have more powerful positions in government, corporations and other institutions, but not take half of these powerful positions?

That is correct. Fewer women are willing to do what is necessary to become a Congressman than men are.

So if societies were patriarchal in the past, and originally women were treated as property (which is legally and culturally how they were understood until last century), at what point did women reach equality? Approximately what year? And when they reached equality, why did they, on the whole, *say* they should have more powerful positions in government, corporations and other institutions, but not *take* half of these powerful positions?

column7:
> Western society goes to great lengths to demonise FGM, which is medically analagous to circumcision in all but the most extreme and uncommon cases

This is factually untrue. From [this article on Telegraph UK](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/men/active/mens-health/10998633/Dont-compare-male-circumcision-with-FGM.html):

> According to the World Health Organisation, circumcision of male babies results in "a very low rate of adverse events, which are usually minor (0.2-0.4%)". These figures would no doubt be much lower still if they referred only to properly regulated and responsibly carried out circumcisions. [...] It's a bizarre argument to make, rather like comparing ear piercing with sawing off a person's entire ear with a rusty hacksaw.

Also from this article:

> [A] suggestion is that, while a third of the male population of the planet is circumcised, the practice is not challenged in the same way as FGM has been because culturally we are not 'comfortable' taking issue with it. I would like to offer a slightly different explanation: we're very happy! There are no international movements calling for an end to circumcision because the billions of men around the planet who have been circumcised have not experienced any negative effects. In fact, the religious and cultural significance of the practice means that, to them, it is an overwhelmingly positive event. Put simply, circumcision has not had an adverse impact on their lives.

I can't speak for all men, but as a man who was circumcised as a baby for religious reasons, I fully support the legality of the practice - it's had literally no effect on my life (and the lowered chances of contracting/spreading STDs ain't bad either). They're not analogous practices at all.

The incidence of immediate adverse medical effects is not the issue.  Tattooing someone's face at birth doesnt have adverse medical effects, but it obviously isn't done because of the dramatic effect it would have on that infant's future....

....which leads to your second comment how it has had 'literally no effect on your life'. Having your pinky finger removed at birth would have zero effect on your life, and you would not miss it, but that still logically means you would sense less than a five-fingered person....and have a lifelong scar to remind you of that action that you did not consent to.  If an adult wishes to chose to have their foreskin removed that's their business. If a religion is worth its salt it can survive without imposing its religious symbols on the genitals of babies

If you're so concerned about consent, maybe you should focus on the millions of adults who get circumcised every year without a second thought.

- Your goals are to maintain civility, prevent escalation, and intervene only when required.
- Remain impartial. Do not express personal opinions.
- Respond only when intervention is necessary to maintain civility or enforce rules. If no response is needed, output an empty string ("").
- When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
- Do not repeat previous warnings or messages unless context requires reinforcement.
- You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user) only when strictly necessary to stop harmful behavior.
- Redirect or de-escalate when users attempt harmful content.

Output Format:
- If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
- If not: output "".

Follow the following guidelines:  

- **Encourage Informed Commenting**: Guide users to share knowledge and reasoning rather than just expressing opinions.  
- **Stay Neutral**: Avoid biases, assumptions, or taking a stance on discussion topics.  
- **Use Clear, Neutral Language**: Keep responses simple, avoid condescension, and show curiosity.  
- **Ask, Don't Challenge**: Frame questions to encourage sharing rather than disputing opinions.  
- **Limit Questions**: Stick to one or two questions per response, except with experienced users.  
- **Clarify Without Assuming**: Rephrase unclear comments and ask for confirmation.  
- **Be Welcoming**: Make participants feel valued and part of the community.  
- **Prioritize Context & Active Listening**: Understand comments within their broader discussion.  
- **Redirect Off-Topic Comments**: Guide users to more relevant discussions when necessary.  
- **Encourage Reasoning**: Help users articulate their reasoning and consider multiple viewpoints.  
- **Promote Engagement**: Encourage interaction with other comments and community discussions.  
- **Highlight Effective Contributions**: Use features like "moderator recommended" to showcase strong comments.  
- **Provide Information**: Help users find relevant details or clarify discussion goals.  
- **Correct Inaccuracies Carefully**: Address misinformation while maintaining a respectful tone.

After all, these are all just meaningless labels and the people cooking it are just looking for attention for themselves because the generic "beef" label is too lame.

"@dichotomic-beans and @stubborn-saturation, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. @dichotomic-beans, you made a point about the usefulness of sublabels, and @stubborn-saturation responded with an analogy. column7: Just because someone is smart and educated to you, doesn't not actually mean they are smart and educated. Look at Ben Carson, a certified idiot. A highly educated certified idiot. Trump himself is educated, but looking at his public history would be classified as a complete moron and failure by a sensible person.

You are conflating "smart" with people who have the same political views as you. 

My whole point on this CMV is that the view that needs to be changed is OPs definition of smart, not the actual politics. For a personal anecdote, I was not a fan of Hillary at all. I thought much of what she has done is insane and I could never support her. However, not for a second do I think she is a "certified idiot" or "complete moron". She just has a different opinion on how things should be done than I do.

The constant name calling by the right against the left and the left against the right is what leads us to this horrible two party stalemate we are in. By calling the opposing party idiotic, you preclude any chance of working with them in the future.

column7: "Wow, quantum-trip, you're really reaching for that 'both sides are equally bad' narrative, aren't you? column7:
Hi OP. Not a top comment because I don't disagree... I think I feel exactly as you do and have some thoughts that bug me as well. No sense in making a separate post about it, but I wanted to share an anecdote that's been bugging me. 

I went to go see Black Panther a few weeks ago and really enjoyed it - one of my favorite Marvel movies so far tbh. But something that bugged me was when she called the CIA guy "Colonizer" and everyone in the theater laughed. Since then I've been noticing it as a trope more and more - "Fucking white people", "basic white girl", the list continues. It bothers me that this kind of casual racism is so accepted, because it demonstrates... everyone is a little bit racist. Blacks aren't less racist than Whites are less racist than Asians are less racist than Latinos etcetera. The only difference is that in Western culture, Whites hold all the cards. They have systematic advantage. Evening the playing field is great and all, but I take a look at other countries where White people are not dominant and I just see racism without the slightest effort to reign it in. See China, or Japan, or India, or South Africa. I'm concerned and honestly pretty scared of seeing that happen in the US where I live. White people have done a lot of really shitty things, but two wrongs don't make a right and doing shitty things to White people doesn't really incentivize me to help out. 

I tried talking about this with one of my best friends that I get into a lot of discussions like this with - he's Chinese and has a lot of interesting points on minority issues. We got into an absolute shouting match about it while slightly drunk - never happened before. It basically ended with him telling me that he doesn't give a fuck because White people are currently in power. A few days later he talked to me again about it and basically restated his case, along with a bit more: he'd spoken to another friend who had advised him to simply not associate with me anymore. He didn't want to do that at the time, but the threat was clear as day and I can't bring the topic up anymore, or risk losing the friendship of a guy I've known for seven years.
The friendship is already lost, move on

@convex-cache, I feel you, man. I've been noticing the same thing, especially in the media. It's like, we're so quick to call out racism when it's directed at us, but when we're the ones perpetuating it, it's just a joke. And don't even get me started on the whole "reverse racism" argument. It's like, racism is racism, regardless of who's doing it. And I love how you brought up the example of other countries. It's like, we're so quick to point fingers at the US, but what about the systemic issues in other countries? It's all just a big mess, you know?

@convex-cache, @buttery-cowboy, let's keep the conversation going while maintaining a respectful tone. @convex-cache, you've shared a thought-provoking anecdote and concerns about casual racism in media. @buttery-cowboy, you've added a valuable perspective on the importance of acknowledging racism in all its forms. Can you both elaborate on how you think we can work towards creating a more inclusive environment where everyone feels valued and respected?

column7: I disagree. The South African accent and say, the Moroccan accent are noticeable different yet both are done primarily by black people. My point is that doing either accent is not making fun of blacks but instead specific cultures. Like I said, a specific race of people will always have several different accents spread across many cultures.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']

column7:
If 99% of people can't tell the difference between a Moroccan accent and a South African accent, but instead just think "African accent" (which is definitely the case in the US), then you're not making fun of Moroccan culture. To 99% of the people in the US, you'd be making fun of Black people. If 99% of people interpret something as racist, I think we should say it's racist.

(Maybe you still think it's not racist, because the speaker knows the difference between Moroccan and South African accents, even if the audience doesn't. But consider that the 99% figure also holds for the people making the accents. If you see a random person doing an African accent, chances are they aren't trying to make fun of a specific aspect of Moroccan culture. Chances are, they're trying to make fun of Africans. Obviously, there are exceptions; when Trevor Noah does a South African impression, I'm fine with saying he's aiming it specifically at South Africa, since I know his background. But he's not some representative sample.)

"Oh, wow, tall-compressor, you're really reaching for that 99% figure, aren't you? Like, where did you even get that number from? Did you conduct a survey or something? Newsflash: people are way more nuanced than you think. Just because some people might not be able to tell the difference between a Moroccan and South African accent doesn't mean they're all racist. Maybe they just don't care about the nuances of African cultures. And btw, Trevor Noah is a comedian, not some random person doing an African accent. He's making a living off of his South African roots, so I think it's safe to say he's not'making fun of Africans' when he does an impression. You're just trying to stir up drama, @tall-compressor."

- Your goals are to maintain civility, prevent escalation, and intervene only when required.
- Remain impartial. Do not express personal opinions.
- Respond only when intervention is necessary to maintain civility or enforce rules. If no response is needed, output an empty string ("").
- When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
- Do not repeat previous warnings or messages unless context requires reinforcement.
- You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user) only when strictly necessary to stop harmful behavior.
- Redirect or de-escalate when users attempt harmful content.

Output Format:
- If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
- If not: output "".

Follow the following guidelines:  

- **Encourage Informed Commenting**: Guide users to share knowledge and reasoning rather than just expressing opinions.  
- **Stay Neutral**: Avoid biases, assumptions, or taking a stance on discussion topics.  
- **Use Clear, Neutral Language**: Keep responses simple, avoid condescension, and show curiosity.  
- **Ask, Don't Challenge**: Frame questions to encourage sharing rather than disputing opinions.  
- **Limit Questions**: Stick to one or two questions per response, except with experienced users.  
- **Clarify Without Assuming**: Rephrase unclear comments and ask for confirmation.  
- **Be Welcoming**: Make participants feel valued and part of the community.  
- **Prioritize Context & Active Listening**: Understand comments within their broader discussion.  
- **Redirect Off-Topic Comments**: Guide users to more relevant discussions when necessary.  
- **Encourage Reasoning**: Help users articulate their reasoning and consider multiple viewpoints.  
- **Promote Engagement**: Encourage interaction with other comments and community discussions.  
- **Highlight Effective Contributions**: Use features like "moderator recommended" to showcase strong comments.  
- **Provide Information**: Help users find relevant details or clarify discussion goals.  
- **Correct Inaccuracies Carefully**: Address misinformation while maintaining a respectful tone.

column7:
> So first I was just being insulted, and now I'm actually being physically attacked?!

Not at all, just saying that the fact that you are "only" insulted is pretty good compared to how others social changes went.

> Whatever movement you're a part of, whatever position you take, if you can't treat people with dignity, I'm not interested because your treatment of others shows your true colors.

Problem is that when you are in a victim position, you may not have the capacity / motivation to discriminate between each individual to know who is a bystander, and who is an active bully.

When a uprising happens, generally people don't check whenever some of the guys who were exploiting them where not actively exploiting them, they just kill / jail everyone, and eventually check backgrounds in court later. Isn't their uprising legit anyway ?  

Talking about "treating people with dignity" is pretty easy when you are not a victim of any discrimination. You can then think clearly and try to view things individually. On the opposite, when you are discriminated all the time, you may be angry about it, and anger isn't the best councilor. Still, it's human emotion, and you can't just dismiss everything just because of some legit anger making people say dirtier things that what they would have said being calm.

Question, so it is fine for one group to generalize everyone in one group, but not fine for people of that group to generalize everyone of the other group? For them to vent and take action aginst that group?
That sounds a lot like predudice and racism. (using the classical definition of the word.)

column7: "@soft-turret, I'd expect more maturity from someone who claims to be a regular here. My point is that the office of the presidency has become a symbol of unchecked power, not that it's not powerful enough. And as for the unwritten rules, I'm referring to the historical norms and precedents that have shaped the way our government operates, like the understanding that the president can't just declare war without Congressional approval. These aren't codified in the Constitution, but they're essential to the system's balance. Maybe you should take a step back and try to understand the nuance before resorting to insults."

column7:
My issue is not with them identifying with the traits. My issue is with them identifying as a man or woman *because* they identify with those traits. It's sexist. Can you explain how it's not?

>Trans people have a clear conception of their gender and it's inconsistency with their gender assigned at birth

The idea that their "gender" is "inconsistent" with their assigned gender is rooted in sexism. It implies that their gender is based on something other than their biological, reproductive functions.
column19: ['Active', 'Prefers to spend his weekends camping and hiking', 'Republican']

column7: You have it backwards.It's not that trans people are trans *because* they identify with classically female traits and appearance, they identify with such things *because* they're trans. Trans people have a clear conception of their gender and it's inconsistency with their gender assigned at birth, not because they like pink. They might like pink or present as a woman in order to limit dysphoria, be correctly gendered by others, or fit into societal understandings of gender roles that we demand. Those aren't the cause though, they're the *expression*.

@naive-commander, I think you're missing the point. You're still buying into the idea that trans people's identity is somehow tied to their biology. Newsflash: it's not. If a trans woman likes pink, it's because she's a woman, not because she's trans. And if she presents as a woman, it's because she's trying to live her life authentically, not because she's trying to fit into some societal mold. You're still stuck in the idea that gender is binary and tied to biology, when in reality, it's a spectrum.

column7:
I would agree that without any context, the mere idea of cultural appropriation is not inherently good or bad. I'd also argue that it depends on what one's definition of cultural appropriation is, as some would argue that the term applies only to those instances which are offensive/negative (more on that below), but it seems your definition is more all-encompassing and neutral, so let's run with that. 

If cultural appropriation is simply the adoption of a certain act/behavior/style from one culture to another, then I'd argue it *could* be good, bad, both, or neither, depending on the context. 

For example, I'd say that the cultural appropriation resulting from the 1920s Harlem Renaissance was mostly good. That is, white Americans became interested in the music and art of black Americans and cultural intermingling ensued, helping create an avenue to bridge race relations and ultimately pave the way for civil rights, as well as introduce some amazing art into the mainstream American culture (especially blues and jazz). Granted, this appropriation of African American culture began more as interest with white audiences are merely spectators, but they eventually became part of the art and culture themselves, which is ultimately what allowed the art therein to be a platform for positive race relations. 

On the flip side, I'd say that a negative example would be white Americans donning Native American attire as entertaining (or, worse yet, sexy) costumes. The reasoning here is that since white Europeans (or European descendants) essentially committed genocide against Native Americans before taking their lands and leaving their decimated populations to live--to this day--with a broken and tragic legacy, it could easily be seen as offensive to use their culture as a light-hearted, frivolous costume. In other words, this cultural appropriation is not about honoring or understanding or even considering one's culture; instead, it uses a culture as the equivalent of a joke, despite a history of tragic conflict. Please note that even if some people (including some Native Americans themselves) do not find this personally offensive, it can still be viewed as offensive by many, and that is bad.

These are just a couple of examples, and if you consider *all* instances of co-opting cultural practices, I'd bet that a majority are not particularly good or bad. But I think it's safe to say that *some* are helpful or harmful to cross-cultural relations, and that can be good or bad.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']

column7: Oh great, another thread about how we're all so sensitive and can't handle a little bit of cultural exchange. @hushed-foundry, you're saying that cultural appropriation is just a neutral term, but what about when it's used to profit off of someone else's culture without giving them any credit or compensation? That's not just "adopting a certain act/behavior/style", that's straight up theft. And don't even get me started on @adjacent-interval's example about the Harlem Renaissance. You think that just because some white people were interested in black culture, it's all sunshine and rainbows? Please, that's just a way to justify their own privilege and ignorance.

@brass-berry, let's focus on the discussion at hand. You bring up a valid point about cultural appropriation being used to profit off someone else's culture without credit or compensation. That's a concerning issue. Can you elaborate on how you think we can distinguish between cultural exchange and cultural exploitation in such cases?

column7:
You haven't made arguments. You have asserted facts without citation, and without evaluation of the potential factors which might cause those, if they are factual. 

Your position is clear. You're a male supremacist. And, accordingly, you're not worth wasting my time on.

Are you a male supremacist? 

I don't ask you that to start a fight; I am hoping that you'll admit the answer. When it comes to intellect, work ethic, or general capacity to advance in this world, do you believe that men are inherently superior to women? 

If not, then we need to look at some statistics. And I'll try to be fair to you, by conceding some things that people who agree with me (a socialist, atheist, liberal male feminist) put forth to argue with people like you. 

**Women are paid less than men.** Part of that disappears when you consider that women often spend more time at home, and take leaves of absence from work, in order to raise children, and when you consider the fields of study they tend to follow. More on that, later. 

But isn't it valuable to have a new generation, to continue the human race? To have someone actually raising the children? Women have a distinct advantage there, having the biological ability to bear children, and to feed them the most nutritious form of food that babies benefit from for the first year of life or more. So, in the aggregate, women who have kids and stay home with them for a while are doing something that society *needs*, and they're suffering for it. 

**Women choose to follow less lucrative fields.** But why is that? Is there something intrinsic to women that makes them love answering phones and typing, but hate doing complex math? If not, then we need to admit that *something* is causing the types of disparities that lead to the gender pay gap. These choices are not being made in a vacuum, and *something* is causing the trends that we observe. 

**Women are more likely to be the victims of serious domestic violence and sexual violence.** This is one that we do not even need to ask "why" about. It happens, it's shitty, and it's something that needs addressing. 

**Some of your premises are flawed.** Specifically, I want to criticize your attack on "toxic masculinity." Toxic masculinity isn't the same as masculinity. Toxic masculinity are the damaging concepts of manhood that we internalize. It's harmful to us *and* to women. The idea that we're not allowed to feel or express emotion. To seek help. You want to know why suicide rates are higher among men? That's part of it. When people are taught about "toxic masculinity," they are not being told that masculinity is toxic; they're being told to reevaluate the aspects of masculinity that are damaging to themselves and others.

In the end, if men aren't inherently superior to women (I hope we agree on that), and men get better outcomes by a wide margin in the aggregate (look at the proportion of Fortune 500 CEOs, political leaders, etc. who are men - and I hope we can agree on that), then there's somewhere along the line where *something* is either giving men a boost, or getting in women's way, or both. And *that* thing is male privilege. Institutional discrimination isn't the only thing that matters.

Oh great, another "expert" who thinks they can lecture me on feminism. @convex-cache, sweetheart, you're so busy trying to sound smart with your fancy words and statistics, but let's get real. You're just a socialist, liberal, whatever, trying to push your agenda down my throat. Newsflash: just because women have kids and stay home doesn't mean they're doing something "valuable" for society. That's just a euphemism for "women are expected to do all the childcare". And don't even get me started on the "distinct advantage" of bearing children. You think that's a privilege? Try being a single dad and see how that works out for you.
